behaviors.description: Wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum
behaviors.justification: This is exponential backoff arithmetic. A real failure would be: 'wait times don't actually double' or 'max cap isn't enforced'. A mock HTTP library returning rate-limit errors is sufficient to exercise the backoff logic. No I/O boundary crossed — the calculation is deterministic, local to the process. Unit test with mocked responses and time tracking (or fake timers) directly catches the bug.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: mock API to return rate-limit errors. Verify: 1st retry waits T, 2nd waits 2T, 3rd waits 4T, etc., capped at max. Confirm that wait duration doubles on each consecutive failure until the configured ceiling is reached.

behaviors.description: Retry logic identifies rate-limit response and computes the correct wait time before the next attempt
behaviors.justification: This is response parsing and arithmetic: identify rate-limit status/headers, extract wait-time hints (e.g., Retry-After), compute delay. A mock HTTP response with rate-limit status and headers is sufficient to test the parsing and calculation logic. No real network boundary — the HTTP response object is fully controllable via mocking. Real failure: 'parses wrong header' or 'computes wrong delay'. Unit test catches this directly.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: mock HTTP response with rate-limit status (429) and Retry-After header. Verify: library correctly parses header, computes wait time matches header value (or default if missing), and uses that for backoff. Include edge cases: missing header, malformed header, header with specific directives.

behaviors.description: After waiting, client resends the original request and transparently returns the successful result to the caller
behaviors.justification: The library's retry orchestration: does it call the HTTP library a second time after backoff, and does it return the success to the caller? Mock the HTTP library to return error on first call, success on second. Verify: HTTP lib called twice, second result returned to caller. A pure in-process mock of HTTP responses will not miss this — the bug is in the wrapper's retry logic (calling or not calling, returning or not returning), not in HTTP library behavior. Unit test with two mocked responses catches the bug.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: mock HTTP library. First call: returns rate-limit error. Second call: returns successful response. Execute client.call(). Verify: (1) HTTP lib was called exactly twice (not just once with no retry), (2) the successful response is returned to the caller, (3) caller does not receive the rate-limit error. Use spy/assertion on HTTP lib invocations.

behaviors.description: After max retries are exhausted, client returns a typed QuotaExhaustedError, not a generic error
behaviors.justification: Error handling logic: does the library throw the right error type after N failures? Mock the API to always return rate-limit. Verify: after N retries, the library throws QuotaExhaustedError (not a generic HTTP error, not undefined). This is a pure code path — no I/O boundary, no concurrency. Unit test with a mocked API that always fails directly catches if the wrong error type is thrown.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: mock API to always return rate-limit (429). Configure max retries = 3. Call client.call(). Verify: throws QuotaExhaustedError (not Error, not HttpError, not undefined). Confirm error includes details (e.g., remaining quota, last response). Edge case: verify error is thrown, not silently swallowed or retried infinitely.

behaviors.description: Full retry chain (initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking) terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely
behaviors.justification: Liveness: does the retry loop terminate, or does it hang forever? Mock the API to fail N times, verify the loop exits with an error after N retries and does not continue indefinitely. The failure is a code bug (no exit condition or wrong exit condition), not a network or OS issue. A Unit test with a mock API and an assertion that the call completes (or times out if there's a bug) catches this. No I/O boundary required — the loop termination is local logic.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: mock API to return rate-limit for exactly N attempts. Call client.call() with max retries = N. Verify: call completes (does not hang) and throws error after N retries. Add explicit timeout to test (e.g., fail if call doesn't return in 10 seconds) to catch infinite loops. Verify error is surfaced, not swallowed.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers share the same account quota bucket
behaviors.justification: Shared quota state: is quota tracked globally (across all concurrent callers) or isolated per-caller? Launch multiple concurrent async tasks, mock the API to track cumulative call count and return rate-limit after M calls total (not per-caller). Verify: all callers see the same quota depletion. The failure is a code bug (quota not shared), testable at Unit level with real async concurrency (not fake timers). No real network needed — async semantics are in-process. Mocking the HTTP API is sufficient to simulate quota enforcement.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: launch 3 concurrent async calls. Mock API: returns success for first 2 calls globally (cumulative), returns rate-limit on 3rd call from any caller. Verify: first 2 calls succeed, 3rd call (from any of the 3 callers) receives rate-limit. Confirm quota is tracked globally, not per-caller. Use real async/await (no fake timers) to ensure concurrency is realistic. Verify all 3 promises settle correctly.

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted (FIFO queue)
behaviors.justification: Queue ordering: if multiple requests are queued (blocked by rate-limit), do they dispatch in FIFO order or some other order? Launch requests A, B, C in that order. Mock API to block all initially (return rate-limit), then allow subsequent requests. Track the order in which requests are sent. The failure is a code bug in the queue implementation, testable at Unit level with real async concurrency. Mocking the HTTP API is sufficient to control when requests are allowed. No real network or subprocess needed.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: issue requests A, B, C in order. Mock API blocks all initially (returns rate-limit for all). Then mock allows retries (returns success). Verify: requests are retried in A, B, C order, not B, A, C or any other order. Use request ID or sequence tracking to confirm dispatch order. Verify that no request is lost from the queue and none are reordered.
